relationship_to_restored_text: Interpretive contemplative layer aligned by verse. This does not replace the source-audited restored translation.

verses.mystical_translation: Jesus returns to his homeland with disciples following.
Wisdom and power appear in a familiar place.
Familiar categories become a stumbling-block.
A prophet is dishonored among the familiar.
Limited trust limits what can be received, though healing still happens.
Jesus marvels at their lack of trust and keeps teaching.
The Twelve are sent two by two with authority.
The mission travels lightly.
They carry only what the way requires.
Hospitality becomes their dwelling.
Rejection is witnessed and released.
They proclaim turning and realignment.
Healing and release accompany the mission.
Herod hears of Jesus and interprets him through John.
Others name Elijah or prophet.
Herod’s fear returns to John.
John had been bound because of Herodias.
John spoke truth to Herod’s unlawful union.
Herodias held the rebuke against him.
Herod feared and protected John, perplexed yet drawn to hear him.
A banquet creates the opportunity.
Pleasure and power open the door to a reckless promise.
The oath expands beyond wisdom.
The request is shaped by another’s grievance.
The demand arrives with haste.
Grief cannot overcome public oath and status.
John is killed in prison.
The prophet’s death is delivered through palace violence.
John’s disciples bury him.
The apostles return and report all they did and taught.
Jesus invites them into rest, away from the press of need.
They depart by boat to a deserted place.
The crowds arrive ahead of them.
Compassion sees sheep without a shepherd and begins to teach.
The disciples notice the late hour and deserted place.
They want the crowd released to buy food.
Jesus places responsibility back into their hands.
Five loaves and two fish become the starting point.
The people recline on green grass.
The crowd becomes ordered in groups.
Blessed, broken, and given bread becomes enough.
All eat and are filled.
Twelve baskets remain.
Five thousand eat the loaves.
Jesus sends the disciples across while releasing the crowd.
He goes up the mountain to pray.
Evening finds the boat at sea and Jesus alone on land.
He sees their struggle against the wind and comes walking on the sea.
Fear misreads his presence.
He speaks courage: I am; do not fear.
He enters the boat and the wind stops.
Their hearts still have not understood the loaves.
They cross to Gennesaret.
Recognition happens immediately.
The sick are carried wherever he is heard to be.
Even touching the fringe becomes a channel of healing.
